coredata.dc:title: Bio-CQDs surface modification BiOCl for the BPA elimination and evaluation in visible light: The contribution of C-localized level
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

Guo, Wanqian
Wang, Huazhe
Liu, Banghai
Zhao, Qi
Luo, Haichao
Ren, Nanqi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doping semiconductors can boost solar-to-hydrogen conversion and the photodegradation in VIS-NIR light, therefore attract great attention, but the perspective of CQDs role is seldom explored. Here, a biomass-CQDs was assembled with BiOCl (CQDs/BiOCl), then served as the visible-photodegradation model for a mechanistic investigation. Furthermore, CQDs/BiOCl removed 90% bisphenol A (BPA) within 2 h under visible light. It was attributed to the C-localized state (CLS) produced by CQDs, which transfers forceless photo-electrons (e−) to generate holes (h+) in the CQDs/BiOCl valence band (VB) under visible light, the h+ mainly involved in the BPA degradation process. Then, the electrochemical experiments and theoretical calculations further proved that the efficiencies of charge separation (η CS) and injection (η CI) were proved by CQDs. Meanwhile, the possible BPA degradation pathways were accordingly proposed, and the ecotoxicity evaluation of the intermediates was also conducted by ECOSAR. The transformation pathways of BPA were divided into five orientations, and the toxicity of intermediates was decreased for Fish (LC50, ChV), Daphnid (LC50, ChV), Algae (EC50, ChV) except for P10 and P12. As the result, this study confirmed the feasibility of bio-CQDs/BiOCl preparation and it could be a photocatalyst to remove and detoxify BPA under visible light.

head.ce:title: Bio-CQDs surface modification BiOCl for the BPA elimination and evaluation in visible light: The contribution of C-localized level

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Urban Water Resource and Environment, Harbin Institute of Technology, Harbin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) doping semiconductors can boost solar-to-hydrogen conversion and the photodegradation in VIS-NIR light, therefore attract great attention, but the perspective of CQDs role is seldom explored. Here, a biomass-CQDs was assembled with BiOCl (CQDs/BiOCl), then served as the visible-photodegradation model for a mechanistic investigation. Furthermore, CQDs/BiOCl removed 90% bisphenol A (BPA) within 2 h under visible light. It was attributed to the C-localized state (CLS) produced by CQDs, which transfers forceless photo-electrons (e−) to generate holes (h+) in the CQDs/BiOCl valence band (VB) under visible light, the h+ mainly involved in the BPA degradation process. Then, the electrochemical experiments and theoretical calculations further proved that the efficiencies of charge separation (η CS) and injection (η CI) were proved by CQDs. Meanwhile, the possible BPA degradation pathways were accordingly proposed, and the ecotoxicity evaluation of the intermediates was also conducted by ECOSAR. The transformation pathways of BPA were divided into five orientations, and the toxicity of intermediates was decreased for Fish (LC50, ChV), Daphnid (LC50, ChV), Algae (EC50, ChV) except for P10 and P12. As the result, this study confirmed the feasibility of bio-CQDs/BiOCl preparation and it could be a photocatalyst to remove and detoxify BPA under visible light.

ce:section.ce:para: Stubborn organic pollutants in water have become an important threat to human health in recent years [1]. Due to the toxic and biodegradable properties, some traditional biological treatment methods are difficult to perfectly remove organic pollutants, therefore, it is necessary to select a green and efficient method to remove organic matter in water. The Stubborn organic pollutants are completely eliminated in different advanced oxidation processes (AOPs), such as Fenton-like[1], electro-Fenton [2], and Photocatalysis [3]. Among these, photocatalysis as a green, environmentally friendly and no auxiliary AOP is a desirable method for water treatment, and it has traveled a long path in technological development over the past 35 years [4–6]. Two-dimensional (2D) materials have received numerous attention due to the infrequent surface and superior carrier transfer, particularly in photocatalysis, such as g-C3N4, ZnO and MoS2 [7]. Therefore, it is desirable to prepare 2D nanosheets and apply them in visible photodegradation.

Recently, bismuth oxychloride (BiOCl) as a 2D layed material for photocatalytic energy conversion and environmental contamination has attracted plentiful attention with a unique layered structure, which is a sandwiched structure consist of [Bi2O2]2+ and [Cl−] [8,9]. However, BiOCl has a limitation on the light absorption with the large band gap (3.3 eV), only has photocatalytic activity under less than 4% UV light [10]. Up to now, some modified strategies have solved BiOCl with high photocatalytic activity under visible light, such as morphology control [11], crystal defect [12], surface modification [13], and coupling with other photocatalysts [14]. These strategies could greatly improve the photocatalytic activity of BiOCl, but the ability of charge separation and surface electron transfer will be further improved. Due to the imperfect interface of large-size material and BiOCl, so the electron transport must overcome a strong energy barrier. Furthermore, the surface defects easily served as a recombination center of hole-electron pairs [15]. Intuitively, Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have an excellent ability for the absorption of light and the separation of electron–hole pairs in photocatalysis. The unique nanostructure could increase the accessible area of CQDs and BiOCl, the intimate nano-heterojunction could greatly enhance the separation of electron-hole pairs [7].

However, the traditional CQDs were prepared with severe conditions, the methods of preparation involved large energy and cost consumption. Bio-CQDs were more cost-effective as compared to the synthesis of CQDs from other precursors, such as graphene, carbon nanotubes and small molecular organic matter. Furthermore, the bio-CQDs had an excellent biocompatibility and their green chemistry nature, which are favorable conditions for environmental and biological applications [16–23]. Rice straw is a low cost, green, renewable biological resource for the environmental and biological applications [24,25]. Although, the recycling of bulk carbon materials from rice straw are widely concerned, but the preparation of CQDs is little known. CQDs as carbon nanomaterial with the size below 10 nm have excellent optical and electrical properties, it has tremendous potential for the photocatalytic applications. [26]. Notably, it controls both up and down converted photoluminescence, photosensitization and the crystal defects, which make CQDs-based photocatalysts by Vis-NIR light for the water separations and treatments. Now, several studies revealed that CQDs modified photocatalysts in other photocatalytic systems, such as CQDs/TiO2 [27], CQDs/g-C3N4 [28], CQDs/BiOBr [29], CQDs/ZnO1-x [30], and CQDs/Bi2WO6 [7]. Due to the small size of CQDs, the interface gap between CQDs and BiOCl will be reduced, thus forming a compact heterojunction of crystal planes. Furthermore, CQDs-based photocatalysts for the increase of absorption range are divided three reasons: the up converted PL, the lattice defects and photosensitization. Due to 0D/2D unique nanostructure, it is expected to have some advantages: i) the planar interface of CQDs and BiOCl provided a bulk-to-surface channels and the capture center for electrons. ii) the highly dispersed CQDs could not block the interface of light and photocatalysts. iii) the range of the absorption spectrum was increased. iv) CQDs clusters can gather pollutants in water on the surface. In the above summary, it is favor to prepare CQDs on BiOCl to achieve the pollutant photodegradation.

In this work, we reported that the CQDs was extracted from rice straw, and prepared CQDs/BiOCl by a hydrothermal method for removing BPA under visible light. First, the physical properties of the materials are tested in detail (morphologies, optical and electrical properties). Second, the degradation efficiency of BPA by CQDs/BiOCl in visible and solar light were evaluated, it was verified that CQDs modification was a valid approach to overcome the limitation of photocatalysis in visible light. Then, the pathways and ecotoxicity of BPA were evaluated. Finially, the possible mechanisms for BPA removal by CQDs/BiOCl under visible light and the critical role of CQDs were clarified in details. Additionally, density functional theoretical (DFT) deduced the coulomb attraction of the electrons and holes to further indicate the CQDs-activity relationship.
Analytically pure reagents and deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm−1) were used through the experiments without further purification (see Supporting Information, Text S1).

5 g straw were dispersed in 1 L deionized water with 1 mol NaOH to pretreat for 24 h. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation and add 1 mol oxalic acid drop by drop to make the pH is neutral in solution, then precipitated by ethanol (99%). The precipitation was centrifuged and washed by ethanol three times, and finally dried in a drying oven, xylan was obtained successfully, the yield is about 30%. After pre-treatment, 0.5 g dried xylan was dispersed in 20 mL NaOH (125 mM) solution, and the mixture was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon Hydrothermal Reactor. The autoclave was allowed to be heated at 220 °C for 12 h, and the temperature cooled to room temperature. The products were centrifuged to separate the suspension, the yellow suspension was filtrated with 0.22 μm filter membrane and separated salt ions with dialysis bags (500 molecular weight cut-off) for 3 days to obtain bio-CQDs solution, the concentration of CQDs in the solution is 0.564 mg/mL.

CQDs/BiOCl hybrid materials were synthesized via hydrothermal reaction. First, 0.763 g KCl was dissolved in different amount of CQDs solution (x = 10, 20, 30 and 40 mL), and 4.85 g Bi(NO3)2·5H2O is added to deionized water. V(x-CQDs + water) = 40 mL. Then, the prepared KCl + CQDs solution add drop by drop into Bi (NO3)2·5H2O solution and stir for 30 min, the mixed solution was added to 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor, which was heated at 160 ℃ for 10 h, centrifugated, washed five times with water and ethanol respectively, and the vacuum dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h to obtain CQDs/BiOCl. According to the milliliters of CQDs, some different CQDs/BiOCl are obtained and marked as 10-CQDs/BiOCl, 20-CQDs/BiOCl, 30-CQDs/BiOCl, and 40-CQDs/BiOCl. Pure BiOCl without CQDs was prepared by the same process, all prepared material is characterized in Text S2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photocatalytic degradation of BPA performance test
ce:section.ce:para: The visible and solar light experiment is equipped with or without UV cutoff filters (λ < 420 nm) by using a 300 W xenon lamp. For each experiment, 100 mg samples were added to a BPA solution (10 mg/L) that absorb BPA in the dark for 30 min to reach the adsorption/desorption equilibrium, then eliminate BPA in the light for 2 h. The experimental temperature was maintained 25 ± 3 ℃ using the water cycle. Each 30 min, 2 mL solved BPA solution was filtered through a 0.22 um filter membrane to obtain the transparent fluid. The fluid was analyzed by High Performance Liquid Chromatograph (HPLC, Waters) and TOP LC/MS (Agilent, USA) in the Text S3. And the experiment of active species was introduced in Text S4.

An FTO (F doped SnO2) electrode loaded with BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl powder was first prepared. 20 mg sample and 0.2 mL Nafion® solution (5.0 wt%) mixed with 0.2 mL ethanol, then ultrasonic dispersion for 3 h to form a suspension. Then, 20 uL suspension solution was doped onto FTO glass and dried at 120 ℃ for 6 h. Silver/silver chloride electrode (Ag/AgCl) and Pt wire electrode were used as reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out at open potential in 50 mM Na2SO4 solution, and the frequency was in the range from 105 to 10−1 Hz. The photocurrent responses of the samples in 5 mM Na2SO4 aqueous solution with or without 0.1 M K2S2O8 were determined by amperometric i–t curve measurement with a 0.2 V bias voltage (the open-circuit voltage of the samples) for 880 s.

In this study, DFT and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) were used to calculate the ground state and excited state of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl models. The geometric structures were optimized using the B3LYP functional and the mixed basis set (SDD for Bi atoms and 6-31+G(d) for O and Cl atoms). Then the excited states of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl are calculated by TD CAM-B3LYP basis set. All DFT calculations were performed in the Gaussian 09 package. The Coulomb interaction energy (Ec) between electrons (e−) and holes (h+) and the overlap function (Sr) for describing electrons and holes distributions were performed by the Multiwfn [31].

The BiOCl, x-CQDs/BiOCl were analyzed in XRD spectra from Fig. 1 a, the as-prepared BiOCl could be readily indexed to the tetragonal phase [space group: P4/nmm (1 2 9), JCPDS file no. 73-2060]. As the CQDs increases, no significant peaks disappear or appear, indicating that BiOCl, x-CQDs/BiOCl have been successfully prepared. Curiously, the (00x) related planes (x = 1, 2, 3 and 4) suffered from serious weakened intensity with the CQDs increased. According to Zhang’s studies [32], (0 0 1) facets exposure model of BiOCl is supposed to enhance relative intensity of the (00x) peak due to the highly preferred (00x) orientation in the nanosheets. Therefore, it was possible to account for the reduced (00x) peaks which CQDs attached to the facets on BiOCl. On account of CQDs can induce OVs on BiOCl sheets [15,33], therefore the Raman spectra of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl from Fig. 1b were further investigated in detail. Compared to the BiOCl, the CQDs/BiOCl showed two similar bands at 63 and 146 cm−1, which are assigned to the Eg and A1g vibration modes of Bi. If OVs were appeared on BiOCl, Eg and A1g occured blue shift in Raman [34]. However, the Eg and A1g locations of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl were consistent, which demonstrate the nonexistence of OVs on the BiOCl surface. It showed that the tight heterojunction between CQD and BiOCl reduces defects serve as an electron-hole recombination center. In addition, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) also proved that BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl were successfully prepared in Fig. S1a.

The XPS probed the chemical ingredient and states of the BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl samples in Figs. 1 and S1b. In the Fig. S1b, two samples showed no noticeable changes except for the increases of C peak. Each element on the corresponding samples was inspected. From Fig. 1c, it had found Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 peaks corresponding to the Bi element in BiOCl, representing the status of Bi3+ in BiOCl for the binding energies at 158.6 and 164.0 eV. Interestingly, the two characteristic peaks had a slight red shift as CQDs were added. Furthermore, Bi(3−x)+ additional peaks were not appeared at about 163.8 and 158.5 eV, it is related to the amount of OVs [32]. There are two characteristic peaks in Fig. 1d correspond to Cl 2p1/2 and Cl 2p3/2, these were observed at 197.4 and 199.0 eV. These peaks also clearly showed an obvious red-shift toward higher binding energies, it may be attributed to CQDs on BiOCl competed for the electrons in the outer layer of Bi3+ and Cl─ nuclei, it weakened the shielding effect in the outer layer for the increase of binding energy. In Fig. 1e, the O1s peak has changed significantly as CQDs were added. First, the obvious red shift was also appeared, then the characteristic peaks of OC-OH and C-OH at 531.2 and 532.7 eV were enhanced and the Bi-O peak was reduced, indicating that CQDs were doped on BiOCl surface [34]. In the Fig. 1f, the CO and CC were both increased sharply, and the new CO suddenly appeared at 285.9 eV compared to BiOCl. According to the XRD, Raman and XPS, CQDs/BiOCl was successfully compounded without OVs.

The microstructure of CQDs and CQDs/BiOCl were analyzed synthetically with TEM, SEM and EDS. CQDs was 0D dimensional dots with the size of 5–10 nm in the Fig. S2a, and BiOCl was a chip with rhombus geometry and accumulation together in Fig. S2b, which is similar to the SEM (Fig. 2 d and 2e). Compare with Fig. 2a and 2b, some dots were discovered on the BiOCl after CQDs modified. The lattice spacing of CQDs/BiOCl observed in the HRTEM image (Fig. 2c) are 0.28 and 0.24 nm, which are corresponding to BiOCl and CQDs, respectively [33]. The results certified that CQDs build successfully on the BiOCl surface. Next, the elements in CQDs/BiOCl have changed through compare Fig. 2f and Fig. S3, the amount of carbon was much higher than BiOCl (from 44.9% to 74.2%). This trend is consistent with the Mapping and Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The degradation effects of BPA by x-CQDs/BiOCl under visible and solar light
ce:section.ce:para:
The above results confirmed that the CQDs/BiOCl was successfully fabricated without OVs. In this section, Fig. 3 showed the photodegradation effect of BPA by CQDs, BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl under visible and solar Light. The pseudo-first order kinetic model was used to describe the kinetic data:
(1)
l n
(
C 0
/
C t
)
=
k 2
t
where C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the BPA concentrations at time 0.5 and t (h), respectively; and k2 (h−1) is the pseudo-first order rate constant under BPA degradation process.

First, the degradation effects of BPA were investigated by different simples. As Fig. 3a, 3c and S4 shown, BPA had not was self-degradation in either visible or solar light. Although CQDs were independent photocatalyst in other studies, but CQDs had a small adsorption effect of BPA in this study (the removal rate < 5% in Fig. S4). Impressively, the degradation effects of BPA by BiOCl were measured in visible and solar light. In Fig. 3a, the removal rate of BPA reached 14%, which was attributed to the absorption of agminated BiOCl sheets [34]. In Fig. 3c and Fig. S4b, the removal effect of BPA was 100% at 150 min by BiOCl and the kinetic constant (k2) was 0.0264 min−1, which is about 31.4 times higher than that of BiOCl in visible light (8.3961E-4 min−1). Due to the UV light in solar light, so it can directly stimulate e─ from the VB to CB of BiOCl. Although BiOCl has no degradation effect for BPA under visible light, but the CQDs is introduced into BiOCl to break this law. As shown in Figs. 3a and S4a, the adsorption effects of BPA were enhanced by x-CQDs/BiOCl due to the presence of CQDs clusters. And, the x-CQDs/BiOCl could remove 90% BPA in 120 min under visible light and the kinetic constant was k2 = 0.0175 min−1, which was higher than that of BPA, BiOCl and CQDs. In Fig. 3c and S4b, the degradation effect of x-CQDs/BiOCl was 100% under solar light, but the kinetic constant (0.0584 min−1) showed significant improvement in 90 min which is faster 2.21 times than BiOCl (0.0264 min−1).

The results were consistent with Fig. 3b, 3d and S5, the degradation effects of BPA were improved with the amount of CQDs. However, the degradation effects of BPA were decreased by 40-CQDs/BiOCl, which might be the excessive CQDs would block the tangent area of illumination on the BiOCl. Therefore, the above results showed that CQDs not only achieves the BPA elimination in visible light, but also improves the degradation effect of BPA by x-CQDs/BiOCl.

The influence of initial pH on the photodegradation effect of BPA by CQDs/BiOCl was shown in Fig. S6. The removal rate of BPA was increased with the increase of pH (3–11), but the removal rate decreased abruptly at pH = 11.01. This may be the electrostatic repulsion between bisphenolate anion and CQDs/BiOCl [35]. In order to further understand the stability and repeatability of CQDs/BiOCl in the BPA photocatalytic degradation, the recycling experiments were designed. The removal rate of BPA remained at approximately 66% after five cycles in Fig. S7. In the TOC test, the removal rate of TOC was 37.6% in Table S2. Because 30-CQDs/BiOCl has a best effect on BPA degradation, it as an example was conducted in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The optical and electrical properties of CQDs and CQDs/BiOCl
ce:section.ce:para:
The optical properties of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl material were demonstrated by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and pure BiOCl revealed the range of light absorption was shorter than 360 nm in Fig. 4 a [15]. The absorption edge of BiOCl had hardly change with the CQDs increased in Fig. 4a, so the defects were not produced on CQDs/BiOCl nanosheets, which is consistent with the Raman, XPS [33]. Wondrously, the additional shoulder-like tail was increased with the addition of CQDs in the Fig. 4a. It was can inferred that the CQDs generates the C-Localized states (CLS) in the band gap, and the optical absorption edge was invariability. The band gaps of CQDs/BiOCl were further calculated by the equation as follows [35]:
(2)
α h υ = A
h υ - E g
n / 2
where ɑ, hυ, A and Eg, are the absorption coefficient, photon energy, a constant and the band gap, respectively. The n value is 4 in this study, which BiOCl is an indirect gap.

As shown in Fig. 4b, it was found that the band gaps of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl material were almost consistent, but CQDs/BiOCl have a BPA-degradation in the visible region. Base on the Fig. 4a, it is believed that the CQDs is critical role for BPA elimination in visible light, which is related to CLS on BiOCl. Fig. 4c shows the steady-state PL spectra of BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl materials. When CQDs was mosaiced on the BiOCl surface, the intensity of key peaks was decreased obviously, suggesting the CQDs/BiOCl had a lower recombination rate of photoinduced electrons and holes.

The photocatalytic activity depends on three aspects, which can be described as the following equation [36]:

where η PC, η LA, η CS and η CI are the photocatalytic activity, light absorption, charge separation and charge injection efficiency respectively.

First, the light absorption capacity of CQDs/BiOCl was 1.28 times better than BiOCl in the UV region in Fig. 4a, indicating that the CQDs improve the light absorption efficiency (η LA). The charge separation (η CS) of the CQDs/BiOCl and BiOCl were measured by photocurrent and EIS Nyquist spectra. In Fig. 4d and 4f, the photocurrent of CQDs/BiOCl was higher than BiOCl and the impedance was opposite, indicating that the charge separation was improved.

Then, some photocurrent tests were measured to cleared the charge injection efficiency (η CI) of CQDs/BiOCl and BiOCl, their photocurrents were compared with or without K2S2O4 in the fair UV environment [36]. Since the η LA was known, the photocurrent was composed with two parts: charge separation and charge injection, which could be simplified as follows [36]:

Based on the optical and electrical results, these results suggested that CQDs have constructed the CLS and the charge conductor for photo-degradation in visible light. CQDs can promote charge separation and injection in photodegradation. Furthermore, the interaction of electrons and holes were further explored by theoretical model.

Assuming that holes are positively charged and electrons are negatively charged, so the Ec and Sr can be calculated by formula (11) and (12) in Multiwfn:

The level of this index reflects the degree of overlap of holes and electrons; the smaller the value, the more significant the separation of holes and electrons. The value range of this index is [0,1], 1 means that the holes and electrons overlap perfectly, and 0 means “no overlap” [37].

It can be seen from Fig. 5 that the optimized BiOCl configuration was basically the same as the CQDs/BiOCl. Fig. 5a, and 5c showed that CQDs are linked with CO and Bi-O bonds on BiOCl surface, and their hole and electron distributions have also changed. It can be clearly observed in Fig. 5c and 5d which the electron-hole distribution of CQDs/BiOCl were more separated than BiOCl, the visualized isosurfaces obviously reflected that the electron (green) and hole (blue) distributions, respectively. The value of Sr decreased from 0.61024 to 0.45226 a.u. is similar to the trend of the excitation energy in Fig. 5g. Therefore, the Coulomb interaction energy between electrons and holes reduces from 4.149351 eV to 3.3522 eV, indicating that the interaction of electrons and holes was noticeably weakened [38]. The calculation results clearly showed that the photo-electron separation and injection were facilitated by CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of photo-degradation BPA by CQDs/BiOCl.
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the mechanism of photo-degradation BPA by CQDs/BiOCl under visible light, reactive oxygen species (ROS) were detected with the trapping agents experiment and ESR spectrum.

In Figs. 6 and S8, a series of experiments investigate the ROS for the BPA elimination under visible and solar light by different scavengers: p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) for
O2 −, Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for
OH−, Tert-butanol (t-butanol) for
OH− and Ammonium oxalate (AO) for h+. The efficiencies of inhibition were estimated to be 88% for p-BQ, 14% for IPA, 9% for t-butanol and 99.7% for AO on the BPA elimination under visible light in the Fig. 6 c, indicating that the contribution of the ROS (h+ >
O2 − >
OH−) was same as Fig. 6d. If the potentials of the CB and VB on BiOCl were adjusted by CQDs, and produce different ROS contributions for original bands [32]. Therefore, it is possible that CQDs/BiOCl has same bands as BiOCl, and CQDs convert visible light to UV light [30]. However, many previous studies have confirmed that UCPL of CQDs cannot appear under xenon lamp irradiation, so the Vis-UV transition is unreasonable by CQDs [42].

Typically, the electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) could reveal defects in material [34]. Furthermore, BiOCl and CQDs/BiOCl was not found g = 2.001 signal which is the typical feature of defects in Fig. 7 a, it directly indicated no defects on BiOCl [40]. On the above results, we conclude that the defects and UCPL of CQDs were not the key for the BPA elimination in visible light. Furthermore, CQDs was not a photosensitive material for the BPA elimination process [41,42], due to the existence of h+ on CQDs/BiOCl. So, the degradation mechanism of BPA in visible light should be further speculated. In this study, the h+ and
O2 − were major roles in BPA elimination on ROS experiments. Strangely, the degradation effect of BPA was not inhibition under N2 condition in visible light, which is inconsistent with the solar light in Fig. 7b. In order to check the existence of
O2 − under visible light, EPR was an effective method by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin trap in methanol and aqueous solution. In Fig. 7c, under the visible light irradiation, a six-line spectrum with relative intensities of 1: 1: 1: 1: 1: 1 were observed over CQDs/BiOCl in methanol, suggesting the
O2 − exist in this degradation system. This result directly confirmed the existence of
O2 −, but it is not enough to remove BPA. According to ROS trapping, anaerobic experiment and ESR,
O2 − is present in the system but not enough to remove BPA. In ROS experiment, p-BQ has competition with BPA to consume h+, so the removal effect is inhibited. From the Fig. 7d, the
OH− is not present in photocatalytic process under visible light. The above inferences proved that h+ was an important active species for BPA degradation under visible light by CQDs/BiOCl.

Based on the above, the CQDs/BiOCl could eliminate BPA in water under visible light, the mechanism was elucidated and illustrated in Fig. 8 . It is speculated that only the located regions of CQD can produce the degradation effect of BPA in visible light. First, BPA was absorbed by CQDs on BiOCl in water, and the electrons were excited under the visible light, but it cannot arrive CB from VB. Second, the CLS were generated by CQDs seeding on BiOCl, it can accept and capture the photo-induced electrons. Third, the alone h+ on the VB oxidizes and degrades BPA in visible light. Finally, a few electrons produced sporadic
O2 − in water due to the charge injection capability of CLS. The above results indicated that the degradation mechanism of BPA by CQDs/BiOCl under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of CQDs/BiOCl after the reusing under visible light
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the stability of CQDs/BiOCl after the repeated degradation of BPA, its morphological structure and chemical character were characterized after 3 and 5 cycles in Fig. 9 . In the Fig. S7, it was known that the degradation effect of BPA by repeated CQDs/BiOCl was decreased 84–66%. The reason of the decreased property was possible to the consumption of CQDs on BiOCl surface. After the recycling, the morphological structures of CQDs/BiOCl were invariable in Fig. 9a, 9b and 9c. In the Fig. 9d and 9e, the intensity of Tetragonal phases was increased, and the characteristic peaks of CQDs were shrink in FTIR spectra. The phenomenon proved that CQDs are constantly disappearing during the BPA photodegradation due to the decomposition of CQDs by active species [43]. Raman spectra proved the invariability of BiOCl structure in Fig. 9f. Finally, the above analysis showed that CQDs/BiOCl has decent stability after the degradation of BPA under visible light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The degradation pathways and toxicity of BPA and transformation products.
ce:section.ce:para: For the degradation path and intermediate products of BPA in this system, LC/MS analysis was performed in the Figs. 10 and S9. In the pathway I, BPA transforms to 4,4′-dihydroxybenzophenone (P1) via demethylation and oxidative rearrangement. Subsequently, the pathways are divided into two stages. First, P1 can transform p-hydroquinone (P2) by CC bond cleavage, and further transform into hydroquinone (P4) and organic acid (P5). Second, P1 can also transform p-hydroxyacetophenone (P6), and further turns into 4-(1-hydroxyethyl) phenol (P7) and 3′,4′-dihydroxyaceophenone (P8). The similar pathways were also observed in the natural microcosm of BPA [43]. In the pathway II, BPA produces 4,4′-vinylidenediphenol (P9) by dehydrogenation, and P9 further transforms to p-isopropenylphenol (P10) via cleavage reaction, and P10 also transforms to P6 via the oxidative reaction. These products were detected from natural microcosm with 13C-labeled BPA [44]. Then, P10 further transforms into the 2-(4-hydroxyphenyl)-propanol-2-ol (P11), P11 is produced to 4-(2-hydroxypropan-2-yl)benzene-1,2-diol (P13) via the hydroxylation and coupled to form P12 through the bimolecular coupling of BPA radicals [45–47]. In the pathway III, BPA tranforms 2,2-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)-1-propanol (P14) by hydrolysis, and then 3,3-bis(4-hydroxyphenyl) butyric acid (P15) was yielded via oxidization [44]. In the pathway V, the hydroxylation of BPA (3–OH-bisphenol) (P18) was detected, this intermediate is major product. First, P18 transforms into the one ring hydroxylated phenolic derivative (P16) and 4-(prop-1-en-2-yl) phenol (P17) via the isopropylidene bridge cleavage, P17 further turns into the P4 [48]. Second, P18 is oxidized to destroy the aromatic ring to form P24, and then the P25 was formed with the oxidation reaction continuing of P24. Therefore, the aldehyde group of P25 was oxidized to carboxyl group and formed P26, and further formed P27 [49]. After that, the P27 was oxidized to P6 through cracking and oxidation reaction, then P28, P29 and P30 were formed from P6 via ring opening, oxidation and decarboxylation [50]. Third, the hydroxylation reaction of BPA in this photocatalytic process and generating P19, P19 then through dehydration, forming quinone and carboxylic compounds (P20). P20 was transformed into P21 via the ring opening [51], P21 transformed to P22 directly through oxidation reaction. In addition, P21 can first transformed to P23, then P23 further converted to P22 through cracking and oxidation reaction [50]. Forth, P18 transforms into P31 via the elimination of methyl group and further hydroxylation [52]. In the pathway V, BPA was reacted to form P32 due to synthetical effects of hydroxylation, oxidation and coupling reactions [53]. P32 was cleaved to produce P3 and P4. The result suggested intermediates could be degraded step by step in CQDs/BiOCl photodegradation-system.

The toxicities of BPA and intermediates were prospected in terms of 50% lethal concentration (LC50) for Fish and Daphnid, the terms of 50% biological decline concentration (EC50) for algae. Furthermore, the chronic value (ChV) for fish, Daphnid and algae by ECOSAR software program (Table 1 ). In the pathway I, III IV and V, the aquatic toxicity of a numerous intermediates was usually lower than BPA. However, the aquatic toxicity of P10 and P12 was higher than that of BPA, indicating the environmental risks were raised in BPA photodegradation. In conclusion, CQDs/BiOCl can well eliminate the organic pollution and biological toxicity of BPA in the water environment.

In summary, the CQDs (about 7 nm) were obtained from straw by a simple method, and were used to prepare CQDs/BiOCl for BPA degradation in visible light. CQDs are supported on BiOCl shallow lattices without defects and the original band gap of BiOCl. In the ROS identification experiments revealed, h+ played the major role while
O2 − had hardly any effect in the BPA degradation. Therefore, the system follows a two-stage mechanism involving (i) CQDs intervened into the shallow layer of BiOCl to form a CLS which used to accept and capture photo-electrons from VB, because CQDs is an outstanding conductor to facilitate charge separation and injection. (ii) The h+ directly oxidized BPA and
O2 − was generated by the electrons from CLS. Due to the lower reduction potential of electrons, so too few
O2 − had no obvious effect in the BPA degradation by CQDs/BiOCl in visible light. Therefore, the main active species only is h+. Furthermore, the degradation pathways and toxicity of BPA and transformation products were also cleared, BPA was divided into 31 intermediates in this degradation system and the toxicity of most intermediates was reduced after photo-degradation by CQDs/BiOCl. Consequently, this article not only proposed a new mechanism for the degradation of BPA by CQDs/BiOCl under visible light, but also it provided a new scavenger and disinfector for BPA in water.
